When  a non-identifying clause does not come  at the end of a sentence, two She married someone. (!) The woman is from Poland. (Which  woman?) to make sense.

If an identifying clause is left out, the sentence may not give enough information on a bus.

She married a very nice young architect from Belfast, whom she met - She married someone that she met on a bus.

Dorota, who does my hair, is from Poland.

- The woman who does my hair is from Poland.

separated by pauses and/or intonation breaks and commas. Compare: no sense or have a different meaning.) Non-identifying clauses are normally would be incomplete without the relative clause, and the sentence would make movements in speech, or by commas in writing. (This is because the noun modify, without a break. They  are not separated by pauses or intonation Identifying relative clauses usually follow immediately after the nouns that they 2 pronunciation          and   punctuation more formal, and are less frequent in informal speech.

clause. There are also stylistic differences: non-identifying clauses are generally There are several grammatical differences between the two kinds of relative In 1908 Ford developed his Model T car, which sold for $500.

This is Ms Rogers, who’s joining the company next week.

than . .. the man over there whose ears are enormous.) She's married to the man over there with the enormous ears. (More natural . . . whose brother drives buses?) You know that girl with a brother who drives buses? (Less formal than .. . whose house looks over a river.) I've got some friends with a house that looks over a river. way of expressing possessive ideas, and is usually more natural than whose in informal style other structures are often preferred.  With (►  633)  is a common Sentences with whose are generally felt to be rather heavy and formal; in an 4 formality mu                                                              mw have heard of. (    . .. asinger-whoseyou mayI   heard.) or He's married to a singer of whom you may have heard,  . .. that you may cases we  use of which/whom or that... of.

Relative whose is only used as a possessive determiner, before a noun. In other 3 only    used    as  a  determiner the-name) not a man whose name I've forgotten (     a man of whom I've forgotten people.

We do not normally use noun + of whom in a possessive sense to talk about He's written a book of which I've forgotten the name.

He's written a book that I've forgotten the name of He's written a book the name of which I've forgotten.

He's written a book whose name I've forgotten.

Compare the following four ways of expressing the same idea.

noun + of which or that... of, but of which ...+ noun is also possible.

things, and these are sometimes preferred. The  most common word order is Instead of whose, we  can use of which or that... o/(less formal) to refer to 2 things:    of   which;      that    ...  of Whose can be used in both identifying and non-identifying clauses.

they were smalL (object of preposition) I went to see my friends the Forrests, whose children I used to look after when the first professional guides, (object of preposition) Michel Croz, with whose help Whymper climbed the Matterhorn, was one of It was a meeting whose purpose I did not understand, (object) I saw a girl whose beauty took my breath away, (subject) object of a preposition.

relative clause, whose + noun can be the subject, the object of a verb or the same way as his, her, its or their. It can refer back to people or things.
